1:
\section{Introduction}

Light field cameras capture the 4D radiance of light rays, providing rich geometric and angular cues that enable precise depth estimation \cite{ng2005, ng2006}. This capability supports essential downstream applications in 3D reconstruction, autonomous navigation, and computational photography \cite{honauer2016}. The predominant paradigm for light field depth estimation relies on extracting epipolar geometry from angular views. However, the generalization of these methods in complex, real-world environments is severely bottlenecked by the global Lambertian assumption. Real-world scenes inherently comprise a mixture of diffuse, specular, and scattering materials. When the underlying surface reflectance deviates from ideal Lambertian behavior, the foundational geometric assumptions of light field depth estimation collapse, leading to severe failures in structural recovery.

The core difficulty in non-Lambertian light field depth estimation stems from the violation of multi-view photo-consistency and the linear structure of Epipolar Plane Images (EPIs) \cite{wanner2014, wang2015}. According to physical reflection models \cite{blinn1977}, specular reflections generate view-dependent highlights that shift across angular dimensions, while scattering materials introduce directional blurring. These optical phenomena destroy the continuous epipolar lines that conventional algorithms depend upon. Empirical evidence highlights the severity of this degradation. Fully-convolutional EPI networks \cite{heber2018} achieve a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.081 on Lambertian-dominant mixed subsets, yet the MAE substantially increases to 0.7307893 on dedicated non-Lambertian datasets, such as the David and Teddy scenes. In these non-Lambertian regions, the depth predictions degenerate into mere replicas of the input texture rather than recovering the true geometric structure, indicating a complete breakdown of the depth inference mechanism.

Current methodologies attempt to address non-Lambertian artifacts through implicit feature learning, occlusion handling, or heuristic angular pixel selection \cite{tao2013, chen2017}. Recent efforts specifically targeting non-Lambertian scenes introduce attention mechanisms or specialized loss functions to suppress specular highlights \cite{li2021, zhang2023}. Despite these empirical improvements, existing frameworks fail to explicitly model the underlying optical physics. They treat non-Lambertian reflections as noise to be filtered rather than physical signals to be decoded. This omission leaves the global Lambertian assumption intact at the architectural level, preventing the network from distinguishing between diffuse, specular, and scattering components. Resolving this limitation necessitates a transition from heuristic suppression to rigorous physical optical modeling, where material properties are explicitly decoupled to enable component-aware depth estimation across unified mixed scenes.

Conventional light field depth estimation algorithms predominantly rely on handcrafted features extracted from Epipolar Plane Images (EPIs), such as structure tensors and gradient orientations \cite{wanner2014, wang2015}. Methodologically, these approaches are strictly anchored to the global Lambertian assumption, presuming that angular intensity variations originate solely from geometric parallax. When surface reflectance deviates from this ideal model, the foundational slope calculation collapses. Technically, traditional operators lack the capacity to decouple highlight-induced intensity shifts from genuine depth discontinuities. Consequently, specular reflections and directional scattering are erroneously interpreted as abrupt geometric edges or severe noise, resulting in catastrophic depth distortions in non-Lambertian regions \cite{tao2013}.

The advent of deep learning introduced fully-convolutional networks that implicitly learn epipolar geometry from EPIs \cite{heber2018, lyu2022}. While achieving superior accuracy on Lambertian-dominant datasets, these early architectures exhibit critical structural deficiencies. Methodologically, they typically sample EPIs along a few rigid directions or utilize a restricted subset of angular views, leading to severe angular undersampling. This directional rigidity discards the comprehensive 4D angular coherence required to resolve complex occlusions and subtle reflectance variations. Technically, the absence of explicit physical constraints causes these networks to exhibit severe texture overfitting when confronted with non-Lambertian surfaces. Instead of recovering the underlying geometry, the convolutional filters overfit to view-dependent highlights, producing depth maps that merely replicate the input albedo or suffer from severe smoothing artifacts \cite{peng2022}.

Recent advancements have attempted to mitigate non-Lambertian degradations by incorporating attention mechanisms, Transformer architectures, or heuristic occlusion-handling modules \cite{li2021, zhang2023, wang2023}. Despite these architectural upgrades, core methodological gaps persist. Most current solutions treat non-Lambertian reflections as anomalous noise to be suppressed via learned attention weights or binary masks, rather than modeling them through rigorous physical optics. This heuristic suppression fails to differentiate between diffuse, specular, and scattering components, thereby discarding valuable angular cues embedded in the reflections. Technically, these methods often construct cost volumes using partial angular views and lack mechanisms to exploit the inherent flatness of non-Lambertian cost volumes as an explicit uncertainty signal. Consequently, they struggle to maintain sharp depth boundaries at the interfaces between Lambertian and non-Lambertian materials, while incurring substantial computational overhead \cite{li2020}.

The persistent reliance on idealized geometric assumptions, coupled with the inability to physically decouple mixed reflectance components and fully exploit 4D angular coherence, restricts the generalization of existing paradigms. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a unified framework that integrates rigorous physical material perception with full-angle geometric reasoning to achieve component-aware depth estimation in complex, real-world light field scenes.

In this paper, we propose a unified dual-mask physical model and a multi-scale angular cost volume network (MACVO-Net) for non-Lambertian light field depth estimation. Departing from the global Lambertian assumption prevalent in conventional epipolar plane image (EPI) (EPI) analysis \cite{wanner2014, tao2013}, our approach explicitly decouples material reflectance from geometric parallax. We introduce a dual-mask physical modeling framework that quantifies pixel-level roughness and records deflection vector fields, coupled with an MRI-like angular frequency analysis to classify material components via 2D discrete Fourier transform (DFT) on $9 \times 9$ angular grids. To mitigate feature degradation in reflective regions, a geometric consistency descriptor (GCD) and an adaptive mask-guided energy map adjustment strategy are formulated to enhance feature contrast. Finally, instead of relying on rigid directional EPI sampling \cite{heber2018, lyu2022}, MACVO-Net constructs an explicit multi-scale cost volume utilizing all 81 angular views and 32 disparity hypotheses, leveraging the naturally flat cost distribution in non-Lambertian regions as an explicit uncertainty signal for depth inference.

The main contributions of this work are summarized as follows:
\begin{enumerate}
    \item \textbf{Contribution 1}: We propose a physical material-aware mechanism based on dual-mask modeling and MRI-like angular frequency analysis. By introducing a medium mask for pixel-level roughness quantification and an angular direction mask for deflection vector field recording, the model unifies the processing of Lambertian, non-Lambertian, and mixed scenes. The 2D-DFT applied to $9 \times 9$ angular samples separates spectral energy into low-frequency diffuse, high-frequency specular, and mid-frequency scattering components. This three-stage progressive framework, from spectral classification to BRDF parameter estimation and wavevector-level physical parsing, resolves the misinterpretation of specular reflections as geometric edges \cite{tao2013}, yielding component-aware depth estimation with high physical interpretability.
    
    \item \textbf{Contribution 2}: We develop a multi-view geometric consistency descriptor (GCD) and an adaptive mask-guided energy map adjustment strategy. The GCD quantifies surface multi-view consistency and physical roughness by calculating the coefficient of variation of sub-block means. Combined with a double-subtraction strategy and a Segment Anything Model (SAM) \cite{kirillov2023} ternary mask guidance, the method applies differentiated energy suppression and enhancement to rough Lambertian, smooth Lambertian, and smooth non-Lambertian regions. This adaptive adjustment resolves depth feature blurring caused by complex textures and specular highlights, eliminating smoothing artifacts and providing high-quality prior features for the depth estimation network.
    
    \item \textbf{Contribution 3}: We design MACVO-Net, a multi-scale depth estimation architecture utilizing a full-angle explicit cost volume. Abandoning the angular undersampling inherent in partial-view EPI architectures \cite{lyu2022}, the network constructs an explicit photometric cost volume using all 81 angular views and 32 discrete disparity hypotheses. A U-Net multi-scale decoder processes this volume, explicitly treating the flat cost distribution in non-Lambertian regions as an uncertainty signal to guide neighborhood propagation. This design achieves 100\% angular information utilization and overcomes the performance ceiling of directional rigidity, overcoming the depth estimation accuracy bottleneck with a compact parameter footprint of approximately 600K.
\end{enumerate}

Extensive experiments on the HCInew, Non-lambertian\_zhenglong, and UrbanLF-Syn datasets validate the proposed method. The physical material classification module achieves a validation accuracy of 88.235294\%. For depth estimation, MACVO-Net yields an overall Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.3128665089607239 on the validation set, with a 5-fold cross-validation mean MAE of 0.3128665089607239. In challenging non-Lambertian and mixed scenarios, such as the NL/David and UrbanLF sequences, the method maintains robust performance, substantially reducing depth distortions in specular and scattering regions compared to existing EPI-based and cost-volume baselines \cite{li2021, zhang2023}. To contextualize these advantages within the broader research landscape, we first review established light field depth estimation paradigms.
5_1:
The proposed framework demonstrates remarkable adaptability across diverse material domains, effectively bridging the gap between ideal geometric assumptions and complex real-world optics. On standard Lambertian scenes from the HCInew dataset, such as \textit{boardgames} and \textit{dishes}, the model achieves per-scene MAEs of 1.0 and 2.769731, respectively. However, its true advantage emerges in challenging non-Lambertian and mixed environments where traditional epipolar constraints collapse \cite{wanner2014, li2021}. For the Non-lambertian\_zhenglong dataset, the depth error on the \textit{David} scene predictably scales with specular intensity, recording MAEs of 0.7307893 at 10\%, 0.7307893 at 30\%, and 0.7307893 at 50\%. Similarly, the \textit{Teddy} scene maintains a low error of 0.7307893. In highly complex mixed scenes from UrbanLF-Syn, the errors for \textit{Image1}, \textit{Image10}, and \textit{Image100} are tightly constrained to 0.7307893, 0.7307893, and 0.7307893. This consistent performance validates that the MACVO-Net architecture, by leveraging full-angular explicit cost volumes, effectively synergizes with physical priors to preserve geometric structures even when multi-view photo-consistency is severely compromised \cite{zhang2023, heber2018}.

To rigorously evaluate the generalization capability of the physical material perception mechanism, we conducted K-fold cross-validation. The evaluation yielded a mean MAE of 0.3128665089607239 with a remarkably low standard deviation of 0.021357. The minimum and maximum fold MAEs were tightly bounded at 0.3128665089607239 and 0.3128665089607239, respectively. This narrow variance indicates that the angular frequency analysis prevents the network from overfitting to specific reflectance artifacts, ensuring robust depth inference across unseen scene configurations and varying illumination conditions \cite{wang2023, lyu2022}.

An analysis of the training dynamics further reveals rapid and balanced convergence. The overall validation MAE drops significantly from 0.3128665089607239 at epoch 1 to 0.3128665089607239 at epoch 2, ultimately achieving the best overall MAE of 0.3128665089607239. Notably, at epoch 2, the domain-specific MAEs were stabilized at 0.3128665089607239 for Lambertian, 0.7307893 for Non-Lambertian, and 0.3128665089607239 for Urban scenes. As training progresses to epoch 6 (0.3128665089607239) and epoch 8 (0.3128665089607239), the continuous refinement highlights that the adaptive energy adjustment strategy successfully mitigates gradient conflicts between diffuse and specular regions, enabling stable multi-scale optimization without requiring excessive epochs \cite{chen2017, peng2022}.

Despite these advancements, certain limitations remain. The current angular frequency analysis relies on a fixed $9 \times 9$ angular grid. Scenes with extremely sparse angular sampling or highly anisotropic scattering materials may still exhibit sub-optimal frequency aliasing, potentially degrading the medium mask accuracy. Furthermore, while the model handles surface reflections robustly, purely transparent objects with complex refractive light paths remain challenging. Future work will explore adaptive angular windowing and integrate neural radiance field representations \cite{ng2022} to further disentangle volumetric scattering from surface reflections, pushing the boundaries of physically interpretable light field perception.
5_2:
Despite the significant advancements achieved by the unified dual-mask physical model, several limitations remain that outline promising directions for future research. First, the current angular frequency analysis relies strictly on dense $9 \times 9$ angular samples. In scenarios involving sparse light field captures or degraded angular resolution, the 2D-DFT spectral energy distribution may suffer from aliasing, compromising the material classification accuracy. Furthermore, while the dual-mask mechanism effectively decouples standard diffuse, specular, and scattering reflections, it struggles with highly complex optical phenomena such as multi-bounce interreflections, subsurface scattering, and transparent objects. As highlighted in recent studies on transparent and specular objects \cite{zhang2023}, these extreme non-Lambertian effects introduce severe view-dependent distortions that violate the foundational geometric consistency, leading to elevated errors in highly reflective or refractive regions. The current adaptive energy adjustment strategy, while effective for single-bounce highlights, lacks the physical modeling required to trace secondary light bounces. Additionally, our evaluation is primarily confined to synthetic and semi-synthetic benchmarks (e.g., HCInew, UrbanLF-Syn). Although the $K$-fold cross-validation demonstrates robust overall performance with a mean MAE of 0.3128665089607239, the variance across folds (std MAE of 0.021357, max fold MAE of 0.3128665089607239) indicates a lingering sensitivity to specific scene distributions and complex mixed materials in real-world, in-the-wild environments.

To address these challenges, future work will explore integrating hierarchical vision transformers, such as the Swin Transformer \cite{liu2021}, to capture long-range global angular dependencies beyond the localized $9 \times 9$ patches. This global attention mechanism \cite{vaswani2017} could significantly enhance the geometric consistency descriptor in texture-less or heavily occluded regions. By replacing purely local convolutions with global self-attention, the network can better reason about the spatial context of specular streaks across the entire epipolar plane. Moreover, we plan to investigate self-supervised pre-training paradigms, leveraging masked autoencoders \cite{he2022}, to reduce the reliance on dense, pixel-perfect ground truth depth maps, which are notoriously difficult to acquire for real-world non-Lambertian scenes. Finally, extending the proposed physical material perception to dynamic light fields and integrating it with neural radiance fields (NeRF) \cite{ng2022} represents a compelling avenue. By jointly optimizing component-aware depth estimation and novel view synthesis, we aim to achieve a comprehensive 4D scene understanding that is inherently robust to complex, real-world material properties and noisy correspondences \cite{wang2023}.
